title: SEGMENT 18 — The cure problem, the latency frontier, and where we are now

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The cure problem, the latency frontier, and where we are now
Let me close with where the field is actually working hardest right now: the HIV cure problem.
Combination antiretroviral therapy, since the late 1990s, has been astonishingly successful.
A person diagnosed with HIV today, in a country with reliable access to medication, can expect a life expectancy approaching normal.
Daily oral therapy.
Undetectable viral load.
U=U — undetectable equals untransmittable — meaning that a person with undetectable HIV viral load on treatment cannot transmit the virus sexually.
This was one of the great public health discoveries of the last decade.
But cART does not cure HIV.
If you stop the drugs, the virus comes back, almost always within weeks.
The reason is the latent reservoir.

When HIV integrates into a resting memory CD4 T cell, the chromatin around the integration site can be repressive, transcription factors like NF-κB are inactive, and the provirus produces essentially no RNA.

The infected cell is invisible to the immune system because it's not displaying viral peptides, and it's invisible to cART because the drugs target steps in the replication cycle that aren't happening.
The cell just sits there, with an integrated, silent, but fully replication-competent provirus.
When something activates that cell — antigen exposure, cytokine signaling, homeostatic proliferation — the provirus can wake up.
Transcription resumes.
New virions are produced.
The infection rebounds.
Estimates of the latent reservoir size suggest something like one million latently infected cells in a typical person on long-term cART.
That is small relative to the total CD4 T cell pool, but it is the reason a cure is hard.
You cannot kill cells that are not flagging themselves as infected.
Existing drugs cannot eliminate them.
Several cure strategies are under active investigation.
Let me sketch them.
Shock and kill.

The idea is to wake up the latent provirus with a latency-reversing agent, force it to express viral proteins, and then either let the immune system or a targeted therapy eliminate the now-visible infected cell.
Various drug classes have been tried — HDAC inhibitors like vorinostat, PKC agonists like bryostatin, BET inhibitors.
So far, no LRA has produced a meaningful reduction in reservoir size in clinical trials.
It turns out that waking up the virus is necessary but not sufficient — the immune system, deeply scarred by chronic HIV infection, often fails to clear the reactivated cells.
Block and lock.
The opposite approach: instead of waking the reservoir up, drive it into a deeper, more permanent latency.
The idea is that if the provirus can be locked in a fully silenced state with extreme reliability, it might never reactivate.
Didehydro-cortistatin A, a Tat inhibitor, can lock cells in a Tat-deficient state where the provirus simply cannot escape latency.
This is a more recent strategy, and is in early-phase exploration.
Gene therapy approaches.
Use a gene-editing tool — zinc finger nucleases, TALENs, CRISPR-Cas9 — to disrupt CCR5 in the patient's own hematopoietic stem cells.
After re-engraftment, the new T cells lack the coreceptor and are resistant to R5 HIV.
Sangamo Therapeutics ran clinical trials of CCR5-ZFN-edited CD4 T cells.
CRISPR-based approaches are now in trials.
The challenge is achieving high enough editing efficiency in the right cells without significant off-target damage.
Stem cell transplant from a CCR5-Δ32 homozygous donor.
This is how the field's most striking cure cases happened.
Timothy Ray Brown, the "Berlin patient," received an allogeneic bone marrow transplant for leukemia in 2007 from a CCR5-Δ32/Δ32 donor.
He remained HIV-undetectable off all antiretrovirals for 14 years, until his death from leukemia recurrence in 2020.
He is widely accepted as the first person cured of HIV.
Adam Castillejo, the "London patient," was cured by the same approach in 2019.
The Düsseldorf patient and others have since followed.
But this is not a scalable cure — bone marrow transplant has substantial mortality and morbidity and is only justifiable in the context of treating another disease that requires it.
Broadly neutralizing antibodies, bNAbs.
A small fraction of people living with HIV develop, after years of infection, antibodies that can neutralize a wide range of HIV variants.
These antibodies — VRC01, 3BNC117, 10-1074, others — recognize highly conserved epitopes on Env.
They can be cloned, manufactured, and administered as immunotherapy.
Trials are exploring bNAbs as alternative or complementary therapy to cART, and as part of cure strategies.
The field has not yet produced a fully sterilizing cure with bNAbs, but the results are encouraging for long-acting maintenance therapy.
Long-acting cART.
Even without a cure, drug development has made enormous strides in convenience.
Cabotegravir + rilpivirine is approved as a long-acting injectable given every one or two months instead of daily pills.
Lenacapavir is a capsid inhibitor administered subcutaneously every six months.
These don't cure infection, but they make sustained suppression far less burdensome.
The honest summary is that an HIV cure remains an unsolved problem.
The reservoir is the wall.
We have tools that can suppress the virus indefinitely.
We do not yet have a reliable way to eliminate the integrated provirus.
The field continues to work on it.
So let me close where we began.
The central dogma was one of the great organizing principles of twentieth-century biology, and the retroviruses broke it.

From that one discovery, you can trace a remarkable arc — reverse transcriptase, the provirus, the integrated viral genome, the origins of cellular oncogenes, the HIV pandemic, the development of antiretroviral therapy, the engineering of lentiviral vectors that now cure genetic diseases, the discovery that nearly a tenth of our own genomes is retroviral, the realization that the placenta itself depends on a captured retroviral protein.
This is a small family of viruses that has had an outsized effect on biology, on medicine, and on us.
They are pathogens, they are tools, they are fossils inside our chromosomes, and in at least one case they are essential for our own reproduction.
They are simultaneously the worst infectious disease problem of the last half-century and the most useful gene delivery vehicle we have engineered.
When you encounter retroviruses again — and you will, in research, in clinic, in the news — I want you to bring back not just the molecular details, but that frame.
RNA goes in.
DNA comes out.
It integrates.
Forever.
The simple chemistry of one enzyme generates all the rest.
Thank you.
We'll take questions.
